_meta.description: Centralised callout text for all Turas HTML reports. Edit text here to update all reports at next generation. Callouts are educational guidance that help non-statisticians interpret results. They appear in reports but NOT in pinned views or exports.
_meta.format: title: short heading shown in callout header. text: main body (plain text or simple HTML). context: where this appears in the report. page: which report tab/page this callout appears on.
_meta.pages_help: Per-module canonical page list shown in the Add Callout / Edit Page-Tab dropdown. Page = which top-level tab the callout sits on. Sub-tabs use a 'parent / child' slash separator so the editor can show the hierarchy. Pages get auto-added when an editor types a new value, but seeding the list here keeps name conventions consistent across reports. Audit source: data-tab / data-*-subtab attributes in modules/<module>/lib/html_report/panels/.

shapley_importance.title: How is the final importance list determined?
shapley_importance.text: The importance percentages shown are based on Shapley value decomposition, which fairly apportions the model's total explanatory power (R²) across all drivers. Unlike simple correlations or beta weights, Shapley values account for overlap between correlated drivers by averaging each driver's marginal contribution across every possible combination of other drivers. See the Method Comparison section for how consistently each driver ranks across all analytical methods.

method_comparison.title: Understanding the Methods
method_comparison.text:
• Correlation: Simple bivariate association (Pearson r) — does not control for other drivers.
  • Beta Weight: Standardised regression coefficient — shows unique contribution controlling for all other drivers simultaneously.
  • Relative Weight: Decomposes R² by orthogonalising the predictor space — handles multicollinearity better than raw betas.
  • Shapley Value: Game-theory based fair division of R² — the most robust single measure when drivers overlap.

When drivers rank consistently across methods, confidence in their importance is high. Large rank discrepancies may indicate multicollinearity or non-linear effects.

effect_sizes.title: Cohen's f² Benchmarks
effect_sizes.text: Effect sizes show how much each driver contributes to the model beyond all others. Cohen's f² benchmarks: Large ≥ 0.35 (substantial practical impact), Medium ≥ 0.15 (meaningful contribution), Small ≥ 0.02 (detectable but modest). Drivers below 0.02 have negligible unique contribution after controlling for other predictors.

correlation_matrix.title: What does the correlation matrix tell me?
correlation_matrix.text: The correlation matrix shows the raw, bivariate relationship between every pair of drivers and the outcome variable. Unlike the importance rankings (which control for other drivers), correlations show the simple one-to-one association. This is useful for two reasons: (1) spotting which drivers are highly correlated with each other — when two drivers are strongly correlated, their individual importance may be diluted in the model, and (2) identifying drivers that have a strong standalone relationship with the outcome even if they appear less important when other drivers are accounted for.

priority_quadrant.title: How are priorities determined?
priority_quadrant.text: The quadrant chart combines each driver's importance weight with its current performance gap. Drivers in the top-right quadrant are high-importance and high-gap — these are the priority areas where improvement will have the greatest impact. Drivers in the bottom-left are low-importance and low-gap — these are performing adequately relative to their weight.

driver_importance.title: How is driver importance measured?
driver_importance.text: Importance is assessed using Type II Wald chi-square tests from the ordinal regression model. A larger chi-square value indicates the driver has a stronger influence on the outcome. Drivers at the top have the greatest statistical relationship with the dependent variable, controlling for all other drivers simultaneously.

odds_ratios.title: Reading Odds Ratios
odds_ratios.text: An odds ratio (OR) greater than 1 means higher scores on that driver increase the odds of a better outcome. An OR less than 1 means higher scores decrease the odds. The confidence interval shows the range of plausible values — if it crosses 1.0, the effect is not statistically significant. The forest plot visualises these intervals: markers to the right of the centre line indicate positive effects.

factor_patterns.title: Understanding Factor Patterns
factor_patterns.text: This chart shows how the outcome distributes across different levels of each driver. Look for clear gradients: if higher driver scores consistently correspond to higher outcome levels, the driver has a meaningful positive relationship. Flat or mixed patterns suggest the driver has limited practical influence despite statistical significance.

probability_lifts.title: What do probability lifts mean?
probability_lifts.text: Probability lifts translate the odds ratios into a more intuitive metric: the percentage-point change in probability of achieving the top outcome when a driver improves by one unit. A lift of +5pp means that improving this driver by one scale point is associated with a 5 percentage-point increase in the probability of the best outcome.

diagnostics.title: Model Diagnostics
diagnostics.text: Diagnostics assess whether the model is reliable. Convergence (green = converged) confirms the model found a stable solution. Multicollinearity flags drivers that overlap too much (VIF > 5 is concerning). Small cells warns when too few responses fall in a category, which can produce unstable estimates.

questions_summary.title: Quality Assessment
questions_summary.text: The quality indicators show how reliable each estimate is based on sample size and variability. Green indicators mean the estimate is precise enough for confident decisions (bear in mind there may still be other forms of error). Amber suggests caution — the margin of error is notable. Red indicates wide confidence intervals where estimates should be treated as directional only.

visualization_guide.title: Reading the Charts
visualization_guide.text: Each bar shows the estimated value with whiskers extending to the lower and upper confidence bounds. Shorter whiskers mean more precision. When two intervals don't overlap, the difference between those groups is likely statistically meaningful. When they overlap substantially, you cannot reliably conclude a difference exists.

representativeness.title: Reading Representativeness
representativeness.text: The representativeness assessment compares your sample to known population benchmarks. Green cells indicate the sample closely matches the population (<2pp difference). Amber indicates moderate deviation (2-5pp). Red indicates substantial deviation (>5pp) that may bias results if related to the survey topic.

results_overview.title: Understanding This Table
results_overview.text: This table summarises all questions analysed, showing the point estimate, interval bounds, interval width, and a quality assessment. The Method column shows which statistical method produced the displayed values (see Method Selection below). A +1 or +2 next to the method name means additional methods are available in the Question Details tab. Green quality means the estimate is precise enough for confident decisions. (note: there may be other forms of error) Amber suggests the margin of error is notable — interpret with care. Red indicates wide intervals where estimates should be treated as directional only.

method_selection.title: How the Primary Method is Chosen
method_selection.text:
The summary table displays results from a single primary method per question, chosen by a fixed priority order based on statistical best practice:
  • Proportions: Wilson score (preferred) → Normal approximation → Bootstrap → Bayesian. Wilson is preferred because it maintains reliable coverage even at extreme proportions (below 5% or above 95%) and small samples, unlike the Normal approximation which can produce impossible intervals.
  • Means: t-Distribution (preferred) → Bootstrap → Bayesian. The t-distribution is the standard method when the population standard deviation is unknown, which is nearly always.
  • NPS: Normal approximation (preferred) → Bootstrap → Bayesian. The Normal method is standard for the difference-of-proportions that defines NPS.
The first method available in the results is used. All methods are compared side-by-side in the Question Details tab — if the methods disagree substantially, this may indicate the data violates assumptions of the primary method.

forest_plot_guide.title: Reading the Forest Plot
forest_plot_guide.text: Each dot represents the point estimate (proportion, mean, or NPS score). The horizontal bar shows the confidence or stability interval — the range of plausible values for the true population parameter. Shorter bars = more precision. When two bars don't overlap, the difference between those questions is likely statistically meaningful. Very wide bars suggest the sample may be too small for that question.

study_level_weighting.title: Understanding Weighting Effects
study_level_weighting.text: When survey responses carry unequal weights, some respondents contribute more to the estimate than others. The Design Effect (DEFF) measures precision loss due to weighting — a DEFF of 1.0 means no loss, while 2.0 means you'd need twice the sample for equivalent precision. Efficiency above 70% is generally acceptable; below 50% suggests the weighting scheme may need review. These calculations assume a probability-based sample — for non-probability designs, DEFF captures only the weighting effect, not the full extent of sampling bias.

confidence_level.title: What does 'confidence level' mean?
confidence_level.text: A 95% confidence level means: if you repeated this survey 100 times under identical conditions, roughly 95 of those surveys would produce an interval containing the true population value. It does NOT mean there is a 95% probability the true value is in this specific interval. The interval is fixed once calculated; the true value either is or isn't inside it.

precision_accuracy.title: Precision vs. Accuracy
precision_accuracy.text: Precision tells you how repeatable your estimates are (narrow confidence intervals = high precision). Accuracy tells you whether the estimates are centred on the true value. A biased sample can produce precise but inaccurate results. Confidence intervals capture precision but not all sources of inaccuracy — particularly non-response bias and measurement error.

non_sampling_error.title: Sources of Error Not Captured
non_sampling_error.text: Confidence intervals only capture random sampling error. They do not account for: Non-response bias (people who didn't respond may differ systematically), Measurement error (poorly worded questions), Coverage error (population groups not reachable by the survey method), or Processing errors (data cleaning and coding mistakes).

multiple_comparisons.title: Multiple Comparisons
multiple_comparisons.text: When testing many comparisons simultaneously, some will appear significant by chance alone. At 95% confidence, roughly 1 in 20 comparisons will show a 'significant' difference even when no real difference exists. If you are making many comparisons, consider which findings are substantively meaningful rather than relying solely on statistical significance.

reading_utilities.title: Reading Utility Values
reading_utilities.text: Utility scores measure how much each attribute level contributes to overall preference. Higher values mean stronger preference. Within each attribute, the level with the highest utility is most preferred. The baseline level (utility = 0) serves as the reference point. Stars indicate statistical significance: levels without stars may not be reliably different from the baseline.

convergence_pass.title: Model Convergence
convergence_pass.text: The Hierarchical Bayes model has converged, meaning the MCMC chains have stabilised and the estimates are reliable. The individual-level utilities can be used with confidence for simulation and analysis.

convergence_fail.title: Model Convergence Warning
convergence_fail.text: The MCMC chains have not fully converged, which means the individual-level estimates may not be fully stable. This can happen when: the model is complex relative to sample size, there are too many attribute levels, or insufficient iterations were run. Recommendations: Increase the number of iterations, check the Geweke diagnostics for specific parameters, review the effective sample size (ESS), or simplify the model by reducing attributes. The aggregate-level results are likely directionally correct but use individual-level utilities with caution.

how_to_read.title: How to Read This Report
how_to_read.text: MaxDiff (Maximum Difference Scaling) measures relative preference by asking respondents to choose the best and worst from sets of items. Preference Scores (0–100) show each item's relative desirability — higher is more preferred. BW Scores show the raw best-minus-worst count. TURF (Total Unduplicated Reach and Frequency) identifies the optimal combination of items to maximise the number of people who find at least one attractive option.

item_analysis.title: Reading this Table
item_analysis.text: Items are ranked by preference score. Green shading indicates items that respondents consistently chose as 'best' — these are the most preferred. Red shading indicates items frequently chosen as 'worst'. The Best% and Worst% columns show how often each item was selected. The BW Score (Best% minus Worst%) provides a simple summary metric.

interpretation_guide.title: How to Interpret These Results
interpretation_guide.text:
DO:
  • Validate segments against external criteria (behaviour, demographics)
  • Consider practical segment size — segments too small to target are not actionable
  • Look at the profile differences, not just statistical metrics
  • Test stability by running with different random seeds
  • Use multiple metrics (silhouette, gap statistic) to assess quality
DON'T:
  • Treat segments as rigid categories — boundaries are inherently fuzzy
  • Over-interpret small differences between adjacent segments
  • Assume the 'best' statistical solution is the most useful business solution
  • Ignore segments that don't fit your expectations — they may reveal something new
  • Use silhouette scores alone to choose the number of segments

how_it_works.title: How Segmentation Works
how_it_works.text:
K-Means: Assigns each respondent to the nearest of k cluster centres, then recalculates centres, repeating until stable. Fast and effective for well-separated, spherical clusters.

Hierarchical Clustering: Builds a tree (dendrogram) of nested clusters by progressively merging the most similar groups. Useful for exploring natural groupings at different levels.

GMM (Gaussian Mixture Models): Models each segment as a statistical distribution, allowing 'soft' membership where respondents can partially belong to multiple segments. Best when segments overlap.

Choosing k: There is no single 'correct' number of segments. The silhouette score measures how well-separated the segments are: >0.50 = strong structure, 0.35–0.50 = reasonable, 0.25–0.35 = weak, <0.25 = little meaningful structure.

weight_metrics.title: Reading Weight Metrics
weight_metrics.text:
Min/Max: The smallest and largest individual weights — extreme values (e.g., max >5) suggest some respondents are being given disproportionate influence.
CV (Coefficient of Variation): Measures weight variability relative to the mean. CV <0.20 is good, 0.20–0.50 is acceptable, >0.50 is problematic.
DEFF (Design Effect): How much the weighting inflates variance compared to an unweighted sample (1 + CV²). DEFF of 1.5 means you need 50% more sample for equivalent precision.
Efficiency: The inverse of DEFF as a percentage. >70% is good, <50% is concerning — the weighting is costing too much precision.

rim_targets.title: Rim Target Achievement
rim_targets.text: Shows how well the weighted sample matches population targets. Green cells achieved the target within 0.5 percentage points. Amber cells are within 2pp — acceptable for most purposes. Red cells deviate by more than 2pp, which may indicate insufficient sample in that category or conflicting rim targets.

stratum_weights.title: Stratum Weights
stratum_weights.text: Each stratum's weight is the ratio of its population proportion to its sample proportion. Weights above 1.0 mean the group is under-represented in the sample and will be up-weighted. Weights below 1.0 mean the group is over-represented and will be down-weighted. Very large ratios (>3.0) indicate severe imbalance.

profit_index.title: Profit Index
profit_index.text: Profit Index = (Price − Unit Cost) × Purchase Intent. It estimates relative profitability at each price point by combining margin with demand. The peak of the profit curve indicates the price that maximises return.

revenue_index.title: Revenue Index
revenue_index.text: Revenue Index = Price × Purchase Intent. It estimates relative revenue at each price point without considering costs. Useful when unit costs are unknown or you want to see the demand-weighted revenue curve.

purchase_intent.title: Purchase Intent
purchase_intent.text: Purchase Intent is the percentage of respondents willing to buy at each price point. As price increases, intent typically decreases. The shape of this curve reveals price sensitivity — steep drops indicate price-sensitive segments.

significant_changes.title: Understanding Significant Changes
significant_changes.text: Significant changes are flagged when the difference between two waves exceeds what would be expected by chance given the sample sizes. Green arrows indicate statistically significant increases, red arrows indicate significant decreases. Non-significant changes are shown without arrows — these movements may be normal fluctuation rather than real trends.

heatmap.title: Reading the Heatmap
heatmap.text: The heatmap shows values across waves with colour intensity indicating magnitude. Darker shades represent higher values. This view makes it easy to spot trends (consistent darkening or lightening across waves) and outliers (cells that stand out from their neighbours).

significance_testing.title: Understanding Significance Tests
significance_testing.text: Significance letters (a, b, c…) indicate which column's value is statistically significantly higher than another at the chosen confidence level. A cell showing 'a' means that column's value is significantly higher than column a's value. No letter means no significant difference was detected. Significance depends on sample size — small sub-groups may show no differences even when values appear different.

dashboard_overview.title: Reading the Dashboard
dashboard_overview.text: The dashboard summarises key metrics across all questions. Gauge cards show Total-column values with colour coding: green (strong), amber (moderate), red (low). The heatmap grid shows values across banner groups with the same colour logic. Hover any cell for the exact value. Use the metric type tabs to switch between percentages, means, and other metric types.

composite_scores.title: Understanding Composite Scores
composite_scores.text: Composite scores combine multiple survey questions into a single index. The score shown is typically a weighted or unweighted average of the component questions, rescaled to a 0–100 range. Higher scores indicate more positive responses across the combined questions. Component breakdowns show how each contributing question performs individually.

how_to_read.title: How to read this report
how_to_read.text: This report uses the Category Buyer Mindset (CBM) framework from Romaniuk and the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute. Every tab opens with a headline finding, then a visual, then a data table you can pin or export to Excel. The focal brand is always shown saturated; competitors are desaturated grey so the eye goes to the client's position first. Every chart carries its base size (n) and fieldwork date at the bottom. Double-click any insight box to edit; pin any view to build your presentation deck.

cep_heat_strip.title: How to read the CEP x Brand heat strip
cep_heat_strip.text: Each cell shows the percentage of category buyers who link the brand (column) to the CEP (row). CEPs are sorted by the focal brand's linkage strength, so the focal brand's strongest situations are at the top. Darker cells mean higher linkage. Non-significant differences from the category average are masked (lighter grey) so the eye focuses on real signal. Look for two patterns: CEPs where the focal brand is category-leading (dark focal column, defend these), and CEPs where the focal brand lags but the CEP is high-reach (lighter focal column with dark competitor columns nearby — these are growth targets).

repertoire.title: How to read repertoire
repertoire.text: Repertoire is the set of brands a category buyer actually uses in the target timeframe. Small repertoires (1-2 brands) signal loyalty or commodity; large repertoires (4+) signal switching. Three metrics matter: average repertoire size (category-level), sole loyalty (share of buyers who use only this brand), and share of requirements (among buyers of the focal brand, what share of their purchases does it get). SoR is the behavioural analogue of attitude preference — it separates 'part of my repertoire' from 'my main brand'.

drivers_barriers.title: Drivers & Barriers — workbook outputs
drivers_barriers.text:
Where the analysis lives. The HTML drivers-and-barriers view is now the Focal brand view on the Mental Advantage tab — it pairs each stimulus's market-relative MA score with the focal brand's buyer-vs-non-buyer linkage gap and labels each row Real strength, Fame gap, Buyer edge or Weak. Open that for the on-screen read.

What this callout describes. The Excel workbook and per-category CSVs still carry the longer-form drivers and barriers analysis: Derived Importance (buyer % minus non-buyer % linkage per CEP, sorted by absolute differential), the Importance × Performance quadrants (Defend / Strengthen / Monitor / Deprioritise based on differential and focal brand linkage), and the Competitive Advantage sheet (focal versus best competitor on each CEP).

How the math works. Importance is derived from buyer behaviour rather than asked directly: respondents rarely self-report which attributes drive their choices accurately, so we infer importance from the gap between buyers' and non-buyers' associations on each CEP. Larger gaps mean the CEP is doing more work in distinguishing buyers from non-buyers. Performance is the focal brand's own linkage rate. Crossing the two gives the four-quadrant map.

How to use the workbook outputs. Take the top differentials as the candidate drivers the brand should defend or build; treat the bottom differentials as barriers to investigate (the brand is associated with these by non-buyers more than buyers). The IxP quadrants are useful when planning a comms refresh — Strengthen rows are the priority hit-list. The Excel sheets are the analyst-grade view; the on-screen Focal brand view is the at-a-glance read for stakeholder presentations.
drivers_barriers.context: Drivers & Barriers — workbook output (HTML view lives on the Mental Advantage tab)

dba.title: Distinctive Brand Assets (DBA)
dba.text: DBA measures whether the brand's visual and verbal assets (logo, colours, tagline, characters) are both Fame (widely recognised by category buyers) and Unique (correctly attributed to this brand, not to competitors). Each asset lands in one of four quadrants on the Fame x Uniqueness grid: Distinctive (high fame, high uniqueness — protect and amplify), Usable (low fame, high uniqueness — invest to build fame), Ambiguous (high fame, low uniqueness — competitors are stealing credit; disambiguate), Avoid (low fame, low uniqueness — retire or rework). Attribution in this study is open-ended unless closed-list is specified in config.

wom.title: How to read this page
wom.context: Word of Mouth tab — bottom callout below the chart
wom.text:
What this page shows. Word of Mouth tracks two things per brand: what people heard others say about it, and what they said about it themselves. Each split into positive and negative.

The six measurements (per brand)

Heard positive / Heard negative
% of respondents who heard someone else say something positive (or negative) about the brand in the target timeframe.
Said positive / Said negative
% of respondents who said something positive (or negative) about the brand themselves.
Net heard / Net said
Positive minus negative. Net heard = how the brand sounds out there; net said = whether buyers are doing the talking or not.
Occasions (Said + occasions view)
Among people who said something, the average number of times they did. Tells you whether the talking is a one-off or a habit.

How to read it

  • The table at the top lists every brand with the six measures plus net heard and net said. Focal pinned at the top, grey Cat avg row underneath as the benchmark. Click any column header to re-rank — the bars and chart re-render in the table's order.
  • The Heard chart is a diverging bar chart. One horizontal bar per brand, with Heard negative extending LEFT of the zero line and Heard positive extending RIGHT. The further the bar reaches on each side, the more was heard. A brand whose positive bar is much longer than its negative bar has net-positive WOM; the reverse is net-negative. The dashed marker on each side is the category average for that side.
  • The Said + occasions chart uses the same diverging-bar layout but for active sharing, and the small number alongside each bar is the average occasions per sharer. So a short bar with a high occasions number = few people, but they're telling everyone; a long bar with a low occasions number = lots of people mentioning it once.
  • Use the brand chips to show or hide brands. Show count reveals respondent counts.

WOM is a flaky metric — mind the caveats. Self-reported recall of conversations is easy to bias: a recent ad campaign or news story will lift heard scores without anything having actually changed in the market. Negative WOM is consistently under-reported (people don't like saying they bad-mouth brands). Bigger brands score higher on heard positive just because more people are exposed to them — read it relative to penetration, not absolute. Treat WOM as one signal, not the headline.

What to look for

Net heard > net said
People are hearing about the brand more than buyers are doing the talking. Often a PR or media spike. Worth checking what's happening externally before reading anything into it.
Net said > net heard
Buyers are talking but it isn't cutting through. Usually a small / niche brand whose buyers are enthusiastic but the audience is limited.
Negative high on either side
Negative WOM is the one number worth weighting, even at small percentages. Read it alongside the Mental Advantage Focal Brand View to see if there's an attribute or CEP behind it.
High Said but low Occasions
Mention happens once and doesn't come back. The brand had a moment but didn't become a habit.
Low Said + low Heard, decent penetration
Buyers use the brand but don't talk about it. Common in utility / boring categories. Not necessarily a problem — some categories aren't social.
Compare against MMS
MMS (on the Mental Availability tab) measures memory; WOM measures talk. A brand strong on MMS but quiet on WOM is well-stored but not being aired. A brand loud on WOM but weak on MMS is talked about but not remembered when it matters — talk that hasn't become equity yet.

Methodology, briefly. Six questions per brand: heard positive, heard negative, said positive, said negative, plus frequency of own positive and negative sharing. Per Romaniuk (2022), Better Brand Health, Ehrenberg-Bass.

summary.title: What this summary shows
summary.text: Headline metrics and the most important findings for the focal brand in this category. MMS (Mental Market Share) is the single most important brand-health metric — it captures the brand's share of all CEP-brand associations in the category and is a leading indicator of future physical share. Penetration is the behavioural counterpart — what share of category buyers have actually bought the brand in the target window. Compared to the repertoire view on the Category Buying tab (which is on a buyers-only base), the per-respondent numbers shown here include non-buyers as zero so absolute counts read lower; the Buying tab is the right place to read loyalty and frequency.

funnel.title: How to read this page
funnel.context: Brand funnel tab — bottom callout below the chart
funnel.text:
What this page shows. An aggregate brand funnel — Aware → Prefer → Past 12 months → Past 3 months — for every brand in the category. It reads as a population-level shape: how big the brand's mental footprint is, where it converts and where it leaks.

How the four stages are measured. Each stage comes from a separate survey question, asked in this order:

  • Aware — the upfront “which of these brands have you heard of?” question, asked once for the category.
  • Prefer — derived from the per-brand attitude question “which of the following best describes how you feel about [brand]?”, which is asked for every brand individually regardless of whether the respondent flagged it as aware. "Prefer" = the top-2 of the 6-point scale (Love + Prefer). Ambivalent / Price-conditional / Avoid / No-opinion respondents are excluded.
  • Past 12 months — the “which of these brands have you personally bought in the last 12 months?” multi-pick.
  • Past 3 months — the “which of these brands have you personally bought in the last 3 months?” multi-pick.

Stages are measured independently, not by routing. Because each question is asked of everyone in the category, some respondents give technically non-monotonic answers — bought a brand without ticking it on awareness, expressed an attitude on a brand they didn't flag as aware, that kind of thing. The aggregate counts almost always nest properly because most respondents are coherent, and Turas refuses to render a brand whose aggregate counts violate nesting (Prefer count ever exceeding Aware count). The three base toggles handle non-coherence differently — see “Switching the % base” below.

Where Mental Availability lives. The Ehrenberg-Bass / Romaniuk Mental Availability measure (brand-to-Category-Entry-Point linkage) is a different construct from attitudinal preference and reads differently from the funnel by design. See the Mental Availability tab for Mental Penetration (MPen), Mental Market Share (MMS), Network Size, and the CEP matrices — that's the brand-memory / growth-potential view.

How to read it

  • The table at the top ranks every brand at every stage. The focal brand is pinned to the top in navy; the grey Category average row is the benchmark. A green ↑ on a cell means the brand is significantly above average for that stage; a red ↓ means below.
  • Mini funnels give a one-glance visual for each brand individually — one card per brand. Use them when you need to see a brand's whole funnel shape without other lines crossing it.
  • The slope chart (default view) overlays every visible brand on the same axes — leaky stages are easy to spot as steeper drops. Switch to Bar view to compare a single stage across all brands.
  • Use the Show brands chips above the table (and the chart chips below the chart) to add or remove brands from the views.

What to look for

  • Leaky stages. A big drop between two stages tells you where the brand's mental availability thins out at the category level. Compare the focal brand's drop with the cat-avg drop — if both leak in the same place it's a category issue; if only the focal leaks, it's brand-specific. The Aware-to-Prefer drop is about preference build; the Prefer-to-purchase drop is usually distribution or price.
  • Double Jeopardy. Most of the variation between brands is driven by brand size: bigger brands win more on every stage. The diagnostic signal is where a brand deviates from what its size would predict, not the absolute level.
  • The cat-avg range bands (faint shading on the slope chart, mini bars on the cat-avg row) show the spread of brands at each stage. A focal value outside the band is a real outlier.

Switching the % base — the three toggles answer three different questions. They are not rescalings of the same number.

  • % of total — raw stage count over the full category sample. The headline incidence read. Each stage independent; nothing chained.
  • % of aware — for each stage, the count of respondents who passed that stage and said they were aware of the brand, divided by the aware count. Each stage is checked against aware independently — preference is not a precondition. So % aware at Past 12 months means “of people aware of this brand, what proportion bought it in the last 12 months, whether they say they prefer it or not.” This is the penetration-among-aware read. It can move non-monotonically if some respondents buy a brand without ticking it on awareness.
  • % of previous — cumulative funnel: a respondent has to have passed every earlier stage to count at this one. Numerator at Past 12 months = (aware AND prefer AND past 12m); denominator = (aware AND prefer). At Past 3 months the denominator narrows again to (aware AND prefer AND past 12m). Each stage strictly sits inside the previous, so the curve only ever goes down. This is the classic conversion-funnel read.

The two filtered toggles answer different questions, not the same question with a different denominator. % aware shows incidence at each stage among the people who know the brand. % previous shows conversion through the gating sequence (aware AND prefer AND every behaviour step). The two give the same number at the Prefer stage (both = Prefer∩Aware / Aware) and then diverge: % aware ignores whether the buyer also said they prefer the brand; % previous requires it. Flip between them depending on which question you're asking — not because you think the “real” conversion sits between the two.

Methodology, briefly. Stages are independently measured (separate survey questions, no skip logic); Turas validates that aggregate counts nest before rendering. The Prefer stage uses the top-2 of the 6-point attitude scale (Love + Prefer). The Category average row applies each toggle's formula at brand level and then averages across brands — not the ratio of means. Significance tests are two-proportion z-tests; panel sampling is non-probability so margin of error isn't reported. Heavy-buyer and frequency analysis (how often buyers buy each brand) lives in the Category Buying tab. Mental Availability (CEP-based memory) lives on the Mental Availability tab.

mental_availability_metrics.title: How to read this page
mental_availability_metrics.context: Mental Availability — Headline Metrics sub-tab — bottom callout
mental_availability_metrics.text:
What's measured here. Headline Mental Availability metrics — Mental Penetration, Network Size, Mental Market Share, Share of Mind — quantify how easily the brand is retrieved from memory across the category's CEPs (the when/where/why/with-whom moments that bring the category to mind). They are computed on Category Entry Points (CEPs) only; brand-image attributes are not included in any of these numbers. CEPs are the operational definition of mental availability per Romaniuk (Better Brand Health, Ehrenberg-Bass, 2022) — attributes describe how the brand feels in mind, CEPs describe when buyers think of it.

Metric formulas

Mental Penetration (MPen)
Respondents linking the brand to ≥1 CEP ÷ Total respondents × 100. Reach in mental memory.
Network Size (NS)
Average number of CEPs linked, taken across respondents who link the brand to ≥1 CEP. The denominator is per-brand — each brand's NS averages over its own linker base, not the full sample, so a small but vivid brand can have a high NS.
Mental Market Share (MMS)
(MPen × NS) ÷ Σ(MPeni × NSi) × 100. The brand's share of all category CEP associations.
Share of Mind (SoM)
MMS ÷ MPen × 100. The mix of CEP associations among the brand's own linkers. Read it as: of respondents who link to anything for this brand, this is how concentrated those associations are with it. Because each brand's denominator is its own MPen, SoM totals across brands can exceed 100% — SoM is read brand-by-brand, never summed.

Show count toggle — what the small numbers mean

  • MMS shows brand links / category total — CEP links credited to this brand ÷ total CEP links across all brands. Category-wide denominator.
  • MPen shows linkers / sample — respondents linking the brand to ≥1 CEP out of the total respondents.
  • Network Size shows avg over n linkers — the per-brand linker base that NS is averaged over. Not the full sample.
  • SoM shows among n linkers — the per-brand denominator. SoM compares the brand's CEP linking within its own MPen-linkers, which is why it reads differently from MMS even when the formulas look connected: MMS uses a category-wide denominator, SoM uses a per-brand one. That's also why brand SoMs can sum to more than 100%.

What to do with these metrics

MPen
How many people retrieve the brand from memory. Low MPen with high NS = a niche brand, vivid to the few who know it. High MPen with low NS = widely known but thinly cued. Growing MPen means getting more people to link the brand to anything; growing NS means getting existing linkers to link it to more situations.
NS
How deeply the brand's own linkers cue it. NS uses a per-brand denominator (each brand averages over its own linkers, not the full sample), so it doesn't scale with brand size the way MPen does — a small brand with a few hard fans can post a high NS. High NS = rich, multi-cue associations. Low NS, especially paired with high MPen, is a “broad but shallow” signal — lots of people know the brand but it only triggers in one or two moments.
MMS
Share of all category CEP links credited to the brand. Compare it to the brand's actual market share. If MMS is bigger than market share, the brand is known for more moments than it currently sells into — room to grow. If MMS is smaller than market share, the brand sells more than its mental footprint would predict — when today's buyers move on, fewer new ones will know it for these moments. Track wave on wave; MMS usually moves before sales do.
SoM
Among the brand's own linkers, how much of their CEP-linking lands on this brand vs the other brands they also link to. SoM well above MMS = the people who link to the brand are loyal mind-share, they think of it first. SoM about equal to MMS = its linkers split their CEP linking across competitors. Low SoM = even the people who do link to the brand don't strongly favour it. If SoM is low, the fix isn't more awareness — it's strengthening the brand's network of CEP links (see the Attributes / CEPs sub-tabs for which to defend or build).

Base: Total respondents (the full category-buyer base). CEPs only — attribute statements are excluded from MPen, NS, MMS and SoM. To diagnose attribute associations, see the Brand Attributes sub-tab.

Cat avg CI (mini bar) — for each metric, take the brand values v1…vn, compute mean m and standard deviation SD; SE = SD ÷ √n; 95% CI = m ± 1.96 × SE. The shaded range on the mini bar spans the lo–hi bounds; the tick marks the mean; labels below show the bounds.

Source: Romaniuk, J. (2022). Better Brand Health (Ehrenberg-Bass).

mental_advantage_intro.title: What is Mental Advantage?
mental_advantage_intro.context: Mental Advantage tab — top intro callout
mental_advantage_intro.text:
Big brands attract more associations to every CEP just because they're big. Popular CEPs ("tastes good") get ticked for every brand. Raw linkage counts can't tell those size effects apart from real competitive strength.

Mental Advantage strips out the size effect. For every brand × stimulus cell we compare the actual count of respondents who linked the two against the expected count if linkage were just a product of how big the brand is and how popular the stimulus is. The gap, in percentage points of the sample, is the Mental Advantage score.

Three reads:

  • Defend (MA ≥ +5pp) — the brand punches above its size on this stimulus.
  • Build (MA ≤ −5pp) — punches below its size; a gap, especially on big stimuli.
  • Maintain (in between) — the brand performs in line with its size on this stimulus.

An asterisk (*) on a cell flags a gap big enough to be statistically real (chi-square standardised residual |z| > 1.96, p < 0.05). Significant cells also get a thicker outline in the chart. Base: total respondents.

Read the polarity of each attribute. Not every attribute is aspirational. "Is an expensive brand", "old-fashioned", "too sweet" — a high MA on any of these is a flag, not a strength, and a negative MA is a relief, not a gap. The maths is agnostic to whether the attribute is desirable; you bring the judgement. CEPs are usually neutral retrieval cues, but the same caveat applies to any cue that maps to an active negative (e.g. "only when there's nothing else"). When reading the Defend / Build / Maintain labels, ask yourself first whether you actually want the brand linked to this stimulus.

Source: Romaniuk, J. (2022). Better Brand Health (Ehrenberg-Bass). Quantilope (2024) worked example.

mental_advantage_methodology.title: Methodology
mental_advantage_methodology.context: Mental Advantage tab — bottom methodology callout
mental_advantage_methodology.text:
Formula. For each brand × stimulus cell:

  • Expected = (row total × column total) ÷ grand total
  • MA score = (actual − expected) ÷ n × 100, in percentage points

Decisions. Defend if MA ≥ +5pp; Build if MA ≤ −5pp; Maintain in between.

Significance. Chi-square standardised residual z = (actual − expected) ÷ √expected. A cell is flagged when |z| > 1.96 (p < 0.05). With weighted data the expected counts come from weighted marginals so the residual is an approximation — a Rao-Scott design-corrected test would be more rigorous but the difference is not material at typical tracking-weight ranges. Bootstrap CIs on the MA score are on the list for a future wave.

Cell counts (Show counts toggle). a= is the actual respondent count linking the brand to the stimulus. e= is the expected count under independence. A bigger gap between a and e drives both the MA score and the significance flag.

Base: total respondents.

Source: Romaniuk, J. (2022). Better Brand Health (Ehrenberg-Bass); Quantilope (2024). Mental Advantage Analysis.

ma_focal_view_intro.title: Focal brand view — drivers and barriers
ma_focal_view_intro.context: Mental Advantage tab — focal brand drill-down callout
ma_focal_view_intro.text:
The matrix above answers "does the focal brand own this stimulus relative to its size?" — that's the MA score. This table adds a second question: do the brand's buyers reinforce that association more than non-buyers do? — the Buyer gap (buyer % minus non-buyer % linking the stimulus to the brand).

Together the two answers tell you which CEPs the brand owns AND whether buyers back them up.

The four reads

Real strength
High MA, high buyer gap. The brand owns the cue and buyers reinforce it. Defend in comms.
Fame gap
High MA, flat or negative buyer gap. The market thinks of the brand for this CEP but buyers don't link it more than non-buyers do. Usually a delivery problem (product, pack, channel) before it's a comms problem.
Buyer edge
Flat MA, high buyer gap. Buyers know it; the broader market doesn't. An awareness opportunity — surface this CEP in comms.
Weak
Low MA whatever the buyer gap. Drop from comms, or commit to a deliberate build.

One caveat on the labels. "Real strength" and "Buyer edge" assume the attribute is something the brand wants to own. On a negative attribute ("expensive", "old-fashioned", "too sweet") a high MA paired with a positive buyer gap isn't a strength — it's the brand's buyers reinforcing a perception that may be hurting it. Check the attribute wording before reading the label as good or bad news.

Methodology. Buyer gap is a 95% two-proportion z-test. With weights, weighted sums feed the test so significance applies to the proportions actually shown. Cells with fewer than 30 unweighted buyers or non-buyers are suppressed — the gap is too noisy to act on. The MA score and its sig flag are exactly the same numbers as the matrix above; never re-derived.

Base. Buyer split uses the “which of these brands have you personally bought in the last 3 months?” question. Buyer base sizes are shown in the section header.

executive_summary.title: How to read this dashboard
executive_summary.context: Brand Executive Summary panel — bottom callout
executive_summary.text:
This Executive Summary answers one question per brand-and-category: "How is this brand doing in this category, and why?"

Category context — eight category-level baseline metrics (no brand cuts), grouped into three sections so the card scans top-to-bottom:

  • Brand reach — the average respondent's awareness, consideration, long-period and target-period buying counts (how many brands they hold at each funnel stage).
  • Engagement — the average number of CEPs each respondent links across the whole category, plus the average purchase frequency.
  • Buying habits — the most popular purchase channel and pack size when shopper data is collected.

The brand-count metrics use an all-respondents base (non-buyers count as 0) so they intentionally read lower than repertoire size on the Category Buying tab, which is on a buyers-only base. Hover the i next to a row label for the base note.

Where the brand sits — metric cards that locate the focal brand. Big numbers are the brand's value; small numbers are the category average. The MMS rank badge shows where the brand stands against competitors on Mental Market Share.

Why it sits there — the diagnostic. The Strongest associations (attributes) and the moments the brand owns (Category Entry Points) explain the position above. Click through to the Brand Attributes / CEP sub-tabs for the full matrix.

Analyst commentary — your written take. Drives the narrative for stakeholders who skim numbers; the editor supports basic markdown.

Drill into any sub-tab on this category for the full evidence behind these summaries.

funnel_relationship.title: How to read this page
funnel_relationship.context: Brand funnel — Relationship sub-tab — bottom callout below the chart
funnel_relationship.text:
What this page shows. The Brand Relationship view drills into how aware respondents feel about each brand. Where the main funnel collapses attitude into a single "Prefer" step (the top-2 Love + Prefer band), this page splits the full 6-point attitude scale so you can see where conviction sits and where rejection lives.

The six attitude segments (IPK 2026 scale)

Love
It's my favourite / I always pick it.
Prefer
I prefer it but I don't always get it.
Ambivalent
I'd buy it if my main brand wasn't available — open to it but not committed.
Price-only
I'd consider it on price — conditional preference, not a brand choice.
Avoid
I actively avoid it. Active rejection is shown explicitly here, not hidden inside "doesn't consider."
No opinion
Aware of it but no view either way — usually a sign of weak distinctive assets or low salience.

How to read it

  • The table at the top puts every brand in rows, attitude segment in columns. The focal brand is pinned at the top with a dark navy band; the grey Cat avg row is the benchmark. Sort by any column header to put a different attitude segment first — the chart and bars below follow the table's order automatically.
  • The stacked bar chart below shows the same data visually: each brand's bar is split into the six attitude segments. Bigger Love + Prefer slices = more conviction (and a wider Consider stage on the main funnel). Bigger Avoid = active rebuff. Bigger No opinion = the brand isn't owning a distinct position in mind.
  • Use the brand chips above the table to show or hide brands; the Emphasise chips below the chart let you fade everything except one segment so you can compare a single attitude across brands at a glance.

Switching the % base

  • % of total (default) — share of all respondents at each segment. Best for headline reading and comparing brands of different sizes on the same denominator. Keeps you anchored to the same base used on the main funnel page.
  • % of aware — rebases each brand's row to the people who are aware of it. Awareness is pinned to 100% and the row tells you what aware people actually think. Best for diagnosing the quality of awareness independently of brand size: a brand can be small but loved, or big but tepid, and only this view reveals it.

What to look for

  • Conviction depth. Love + Prefer is the conviction bar — the Prefer stage on the main funnel. Big brands get conviction by virtue of size; the diagnostic signal is when a small brand has unusually high Love + Prefer (a niche with hard fans) or a big brand has unusually low (broad reach but no commitment).
  • Rejection. Even small Avoid percentages are strategically loaded — rejection is active, not passive. Probe rejection drivers in the Mental Advantage Focal brand view (look for the Fame gap and Weak labels) and the workbook's Importance × Performance sheet.
  • No opinion. A big No opinion bar means awareness exists but the brand isn't standing for anything in mind. Treat it as latent untapped buyers if Distinctive Brand Assets are weak, or wasted advertising spend if they're strong.
  • % aware view. Flip to % aware to compare conversion quality when brand sizes differ a lot. The denominator changes per brand but each row reads as 100%.

Methodology, briefly. Attitude is asked once per brand on the 6-point IPK 2026 preference scale (Love / Prefer / Ambivalent / Price-only / Avoid / No opinion). Love + Prefer feed the "Prefer" stage on the main funnel; the other four segments are excluded from Consider. The pooled Cat avg row uses sum-of-counts ÷ sum-of-aware-bases, not an unweighted mean of brand-level fractions, so categories with one tiny-base brand don't blow up to > 100%.

mental_availability_attributes.title: How to read this page
mental_availability_attributes.context: Mental Availability — Brand Attributes sub-tab — bottom callout
mental_availability_attributes.text:
What is a brand attribute? A brand attribute is a perception statement — the image, character, or quality respondents associate with a brand (e.g. good value, premium quality, for families, fun). Attributes describe how the brand feels in respondents' minds. They are different from CEPs (the next sub-tab), which describe when buyers retrieve the category from memory.

How the question is asked. Attribute statements are designed by the analyst. Each statement is shown once to every respondent, who then free-associates by picking the brands (one, several, or none) that come to mind for that statement — not the other way round. So a cell of 0% means no respondent linked that brand to that statement, which is itself a signal.

Mind the polarity. Not every attribute is aspirational. "Is an expensive brand", "old-fashioned", "too sweet" — a high score on these is a flag, not a strength. Always ask whether the brand wants to own this association before reading the cell. The chart and Mental Advantage tab treat all attributes the same mathematically; the desirability call sits with you.

What this page shows. A brand × attribute matrix. Each cell is the percentage of respondents who linked the brand (column) to the attribute (row). The focal brand is pinned to the first column with a dark navy band; the Cat avg column is the benchmark across all brands.

How to read it

  • Sort by any column header to put a different brand first — the dot-plot chart below follows the table's row order so the two views stay aligned.
  • The chart draws one row per attribute; each brand is a coloured dot, the cat-avg is a dashed grey line. Use it to spot attributes where the focal sits well above or below the spread.
  • Use the Show brands chips above the table to filter which brands appear in both the matrix and the chart.
  • Toggle Show heatmap for a red/white/blue divergence shading that highlights cells significantly above (blue) or below (red) the row average.
  • Toggle Show count to read the absolute respondent count in each cell instead of just the percentage.

Switching the % base

  • % of total (default) — share of all respondents who linked the brand to the attribute. Best for headline reading; the cat-avg is meaningful across brands of any size.
  • % of aware — rebases each brand's column to respondents who are aware of it. Pins awareness at 100% and shows attribute strength among those who know the brand. Best for diagnosing a small brand's image quality independently of its size.

What to look for

  • Attributes the focal owns. Cells where the focal column is significantly above the cat-avg (sig arrow, blue heat) tell you what the brand stands for. Defend these — they're the source of distinctiveness.
  • Attributes the focal lags. Cells significantly below cat-avg are gaps. Decide whether to close the gap (relevance) or accept it (deliberate positioning).
  • Double Jeopardy applies here too. Bigger brands attract more attribute associations on every row just because more people know them. The diagnostic signal is where the focal deviates from what its size predicts — the Mental Advantage tab quantifies this directly.
  • No-opinion. Attributes where every brand scores low usually mean the statement isn't resonating — rewrite it next wave.

Methodology, briefly. Attribute statements are perception items, not category entry points. The matrix uses total-sample base by default; small bases (n < 30) are dimmed. Significance is a two-proportion z-test against the row's cat-avg.

mental_availability_ceps.title: How to read this page
mental_availability_ceps.context: Mental Availability — Category Entry Points sub-tab — bottom callout
mental_availability_ceps.text:
What is a Category Entry Point (CEP)? A CEP is a retrieval cue — one of the when, where, why, with whom, with what, how moments that bring the category to mind for a buyer. CEPs are broader than “buying situations”; they include occasions, motives, emotional states, contexts, and triggers. Examples in food: quick weeknight dinner, Sunday lunch, kids' lunchbox, entertaining guests, need an energy boost, making something special. The brand that comes to mind across more CEPs in more buyers wins more often. CEPs come from the Ehrenberg-Bass framework (Romaniuk, Better Brand Health, 2022) and are the operational definition of mental availability.

How CEPs differ from attributes. Attributes describe how the brand feels (premium, fun, good value). CEPs describe when buyers retrieve the category, and which brand they retrieve at that moment. In the Romaniuk framework only the CEP-brand network drives the headline mental-availability metrics — Mental Penetration, Network Size, Mental Market Share and Share of Mind on the Headline Metrics tab. Attributes feed the diagnostic views (Mental Advantage, Drivers & Barriers) but are not in those headline numbers.

How the question is asked. CEP statements are designed in collaboration with the analyst to cover the category's main retrieval moments. Each CEP is shown once to every respondent, who free-associates by picking the brands (one, several, or none) that come to mind for that cue.

What this page shows. A brand × CEP matrix. Each cell is the percentage of respondents who linked the brand (column) to the CEP (row). The focal brand is pinned to the first column with a dark navy band; the Cat avg column is the benchmark.

How to read it

  • Sort by any column header to re-rank rows — the dot-plot chart below follows the table's order.
  • The chart draws one row per CEP; each brand is a coloured dot, the cat-avg is a dashed grey line. CEPs where the focal dot sits clearly to the right of the cat-avg line are situations the brand owns.
  • Use the Show brands chips to filter the matrix and chart together.
  • Show heatmap applies a divergence shading so cells significantly above the row average are blue, below are red.
  • Show count reveals the absolute respondent count in each cell.

Switching the % base

  • % of total (default) — the classic MMS denominator. Share of all respondents who link the brand to the CEP. Best for headline reading and direct comparison across brands.
  • % of aware — rebases to respondents aware of the brand. Pins awareness at 100% and shows linkage strength among those who know the brand. Best for asking "of those who know my brand, do they think of me at this moment?"

What to look for

  • CEPs the focal owns. Rows where the focal column outpaces every other — defend these. They're the moments where the brand reliably comes to mind first.
  • High-reach CEPs the focal lags. CEPs with high cat-avg but low focal — these are growth targets. Broadening associations to a high-reach CEP is the EBI growth lever.
  • Network breadth. Look across the whole focal column: a brand linked to many CEPs has higher Network Size (NS) and is mentally available in more situations. Small brands often link to few CEPs — broadening, not deepening, is the priority.
  • Double Jeopardy. Bigger brands attract more CEP linkages everywhere just because more people know them. The diagnostic signal is where the focal deviates from what size predicts — quantified in the Mental Advantage sub-tab.

Methodology, briefly. CEPs are designed in collaboration with the analyst to cover the category's main retrieval moments (the W-questions: when, where, why, with whom, with what, how). The matrix uses total-sample base by default. Significance is a two-proportion z-test against the row's cat-avg. Small bases (n < 30) are dimmed. The headline MMS / MPen / Network Size metrics on the Headline Metrics tab are computed from this matrix.

cat_buying_context.title: How to read this page
cat_buying_context.context: Cat Buying — Category Context sub-tab — bottom callout
cat_buying_context.text:
What this page shows. The shape of the category, not any one brand. Three things sit on this tab: the per-buyer KPIs (avg purchases and avg brands bought), the category-wide purchase-frequency split, and where people shop the category. Together they answer “what does buying in this category actually look like?” for the focal-category respondents in this study.

Bases — mind two of them on this tab.

  • The two KPIs (avg purchases / cat buyer and avg brands bought / cat buyer) and the category frequency / repertoire distributions are computed across past-3-month category buyers — anyone who said yes to at least one brand on the “Which of these … brands have you personally bought in the last 3 months?” question. Respondents who didn’t buy in the past 3 months are excluded.
  • The Where category respondents shop chart uses a different base: all focal-cat respondents for this category — not just past-3-month buyers. Channel use was asked of everyone routed to this category.

How the KPIs are calculated.

Avg purchases / category buyer
For each buyer, add up their answers to “How many times have you bought each of these brands in the last 3 months?” across all the brands they reported buying. The question explicitly instructs respondents to count each brand separately even if they bought several on a single shopping trip, so this is a count of brand-purchase events, not trips. Average that total across all past-3-month category buyers. A buyer who bought Brand A three times and Brand B twice contributes 5; non-buyers are excluded from the average rather than counted as 0.
Avg brands bought / category buyer
Average number of distinct brands per past-3-month category buyer. Counts brands, not trips. A buyer who bought Brand A three times and Brand B twice contributes 2.

Where category respondents shop. The question was “Where did you buy …?” with the listed channel options. Bars are sorted by share descending so the most-used channel reads at the top. The question is multi-mention — respondents could pick more than one place — so the bars sum to more than 100%. Labels come from the channel option list in the Survey Structure; if a label looks like a raw code (e.g. SPMKT instead of Supermarket) the structure file is missing a display name for that option.

Category frequency distribution. The 1 / 2 / 3–5 / 6+ buckets count total category purchases per buyer (the same totals used for Avg purchases above) — not per brand. Heavy-category-buyer share at the right; light-category-buyer share at the left. The Buyer Heaviness sub-tab uses the same totals to build the tertile split for per-brand profiles.

cat_buying_brand_summary.title: How to read this page
cat_buying_brand_summary.context: Cat Buying — Brand Summary sub-tab — bottom callout below the chart
cat_buying_brand_summary.text:
What this page shows. A one-row-per-brand performance summary for the focal category, computed from the per-brand purchase counts (the “how many times have you bought each of these brands in the last 3 months?” question, reconciled against the “which of these brands have you personally bought in the last 3 months?” multi-pick). Every metric here uses the same base — all screened category respondents, including lapsed and zero-purchase — so brands can be compared on like-for-like footing. The Loyalty Segmentation tab uses a tighter cat-buyer base, which is why its “% Cat buyers” reads higher than Penetration here for the same brand.

Columns explained

Brand buyers (n=)
Weighted respondent count who bought this brand in the past 3 months, after reconciling the per-brand purchase counts against the past-3-month multi-pick. The denominator behind the brand's penetration value.
Penetration
% of all screened category respondents who bought this brand in the past 3 months. Reach in physical purchase. May differ from the Brand Funnel by 1–3pp because the reconciliation pass promotes respondents who reported a per-brand purchase count but didn't tick the brand on the past-3-month multi-pick.
Avg purchases
Mean number of times the brand was bought, averaged over the brand's buyers (not over the full sample). Higher Avg purchases means the brand's buyers come back more often.
Volume share
The brand's estimated share of category purchase volume. Computed as (Penetration × Avg purchases) ÷ category mean purchases. Brands not in this list account for any shortfall from 100%.
Loyalty (SCR)
Share of Category Requirement — among this brand's buyers, the % of their total category purchases that go to this brand. SCR is a per-brand loyalty metric, so values do not sum to 100% across brands — each brand's SCR is measured within its own buyer group.

How to read it

  • The focal brand is pinned at the top of the table with a navy band; other brands sort by Penetration desc by default. Click any column header to re-sort — the chart below re-renders in the table's order.
  • The Cat avg row shows the mean across brands plus a CI mini-bar (m ± 1 SD across brand values). The shaded range is the cross-brand dispersion band; the tick marks the mean.
  • Show heatmap colours every cell by its position in the cat-avg band: green = above +1 SD, red = below −1 SD, amber = within. This is a cross-brand dispersion band, not a sampling confidence interval on the estimate itself.
  • Show chart reveals a horizontal bar chart for the column you pick. The dashed grey vertical line on each bar marks the category average for that column — brands whose bar crosses the line outperform the average; those that don't are below.

What to look for

  • Bigger brands win every column. That's Double Jeopardy — absolute numbers aren't the read; the heat-map is. A focal that's green on Loyalty (SCR) but mid on Penetration is a small brand with hard fans. Green on Penetration but red on Loyalty is a broad brand that nobody really commits to.
  • Penetration here vs the Loyalty tab's “% Cat buyers”. The Penetration number for the same brand always reads lower here, because this base includes lapsed and zero-purchase respondents while the Loyalty tab counts only past-3-month category buyers. The size of the gap is the lapsed-buyer pool.
  • Volume share vs Penetration. The gap between these two tells you about buyer heaviness. Volume share well above Penetration = the brand's buyers buy a lot. Volume share well below Penetration = lots of light buyers. The Purchase Distribution tab shows the underlying frequency.

cat_buying_loyalty.title: How to read this page
cat_buying_loyalty.context: Cat Buying — Loyalty Segmentation sub-tab — bottom callout below the chart
cat_buying_loyalty.text:
What this page shows. Every brand's category buyers segmented by loyalty over the past 3 months (target purchase window): Sole, Primary, Secondary, or Not bought. The base is category buyers (respondents who said yes to at least one brand on the “bought in the last 3 months” question) — respondents who bought something in the category, not the full sample. That's why the table's “% Cat buyers” reads higher than the Brand Summary's Penetration: lapsed and zero-purchase respondents are excluded.

The four segments

Sole buyer
Bought this brand and no other brand in the category in the past 3 months.
Primary (>50% SCR)
This brand is more than half of the buyer's category purchases over the past 3 months — they buy other brands too but this one is dominant.
Secondary (≤50%)
Bought this brand in the past 3 months, but another brand takes the majority of their category spend.
Not bought
Category buyer who did not buy this brand in the past 3 months. Lapsed or never-bought.

How to read it

  • The table places brands as rows; the four segments as columns. The focal brand is pinned in navy. Click a column header to re-rank — the chart re-renders in the table's order.
  • The % Cat buyers column equals Sole + Primary + Secondary — the brand's reach among category buyers over the past 3 months. With Show count on, the brand's buyer count appears under the percentage.
  • The stacked bar chart below shows each brand's mix across the four segments. Emphasise chips fade everything except the chosen segment so you can compare a single segment across brands at a glance.

What to look for

  • Sole + Primary share. A brand with a big Sole + Primary slice has buyers who go to it first. A brand carried mostly by its Secondary slice is one people buy alongside other brands but rarely as the main choice.
  • Not bought. The share of category buyers who didn't pick this brand in the past 3 months. The size of the addressable pool inside the category.
  • Compare to the Brand Summary SCR. Same loyalty story told two ways: SCR over there is one number; the four segments here show whether that loyalty sits with Sole-loyalists or is spread across Primary and Secondary buyers.

cat_buying_dist.title: How to read this page
cat_buying_dist.context: Cat Buying — Purchase Distribution sub-tab — bottom callout below the chart
cat_buying_dist.text:
What this page shows. Every brand's buyers split by purchase frequency over the past 3 months (target purchase window). Each row tells you, of this brand's buyers, what proportion bought it once, twice, three-to-five times, or six or more times. The base is brand buyers — respondents who bought this brand at least once in the past 3 months — so each row sums to 100%.

The four frequency buckets (default labels)

Light (1×)
Bought the brand exactly once in the past 3 months.
Moderate (2×)
Bought it twice.
Regular (3–5×)
Bought it three to five times.
Frequent (6+×)
Bought it six or more times. Heavy buyers.

How to read it

  • The % Cat buyers column shows what share of category buyers bought this brand at all in the past 3 months. With Show count on, the brand's buyer count appears under the percentage — this is the denominator behind every cell in the row.
  • The stacked bar chart below visualises the distribution: a brand whose bar leans right (more Regular + Frequent) has heavier buyers; a brand whose bar leans left has lighter buyers.
  • The Cat avg row shows the mean across brands plus a ±1 SD CI mini-bar.

What to look for

  • A flat-looking distribution is normal. Categories follow the Dirichlet pattern — far more light buyers than heavy, and most brands carry roughly the same shape. If a brand's bar looks much like the category average, that is the typical mass-brand profile, not a flag.
  • A heavy skew is the niche pattern, not a sign of strength. A brand with a tall Frequent (6+×) bar is being picked deliberately by a small group of dedicated users. Sharp's Natural Monopoly Law goes the other way: the bigger category brands over-recruit light category buyers, not heavy ones. Growth comes from acquiring more light buyers, not from squeezing more out of the heavy ones.
  • Cross-check against the Buyer Heaviness tab. This chart shows how often a brand's buyers buy that brand. Heaviness shows how heavy they are in the category overall. A brand can look light on this tab and still over-recruit heavy category buyers — or the reverse. Read the two together.

cat_buying_dop.title: How to read this page
cat_buying_dop.context: Cat Buying — Duplication of Purchase sub-tab — bottom callout
cat_buying_dop.text:
What this page shows. A brand × brand matrix of shared buyers over the past 3 months. Read across a row: of this brand's buyers, what % also bought each column brand. The diagonal (brand × itself) is dashed — not meaningful. The Category-average row at the bottom is the mean duplication down each column across the other brands, with a ±1 SD mini-bar showing cross-brand spread.

How expected duplication is calculated — in plain English. Ehrenberg observed across hundreds of categories that brand sharing follows a simple rule: if you take any brand's buyers and look at who else they buy, the % who buy a particular partner brand depends mostly on how big that partner is, not on which brand the buyers came from.

The formula: expected sharing = D × partner's penetration. So if Brand Y is bought by 40% of category buyers and the category's D is 1.4, then about 56% (= 1.4 × 40%) of any other brand's buyers should also buy Brand Y. The prediction doesn't depend on which brand you started with — only on how big Y is and how much switching the category does overall.

Where D comes from. D is the slope of the line that best fits the cloud of (observed sharing, partner penetration) points across every off-diagonal cell in the matrix, forced through zero. Higher D = the category does more switching overall (buyers spread their purchases around). Lower D = brands are stickier than average. D is typically between 1 and 3 for FMCG.

Two consequences worth keeping in mind:

  • Bigger brands appear in more rows simply because they have more buyers. That's not stealing — it's arithmetic.
  • The interesting reading isn't the absolute % in a cell. It's the deviation: how far above or below the law's prediction is each cell.

Two viewing modes

Observed
Raw % of row-brand buyers who also bought each column brand. Cells colour against the cross-brand ±1 SD band on each column (green above, red below, amber within).
Deviations
Observed minus predicted (actual − D × partner penetration) in percentage points. The partition-finding view: large positive deviations (e.g. +5pp or more) flag pairs sharing above the law's prediction; large negatives flag pairs sharing less than expected.

The toolbar chips

  • D = duplication coefficient (above). Higher = more switching across the category; lower = stickier brands.
  • MAD = mean absolute deviation across the off-diagonal cells. Closer to 0 = the DoP law fits the whole category well (a single mass-market). Larger values mean sub-segments or partitions are hiding inside.

The Partition partners / rivals card. Sits below the matrix for the focal brand. It does something slightly different from the Deviations view: it compares the focal's row to the rest of the matrix, not to the law's prediction.

For each non-focal brand B, the card takes the average of column B down all the other brand rows (the “typical” sharing rate other brands have with B). Then it asks: does focal share with B more or less than that?

  • Partners = top 3 brands where focal's sharing is highest relative to the column average. These are the brands focal cohabits with more than other brands do. Format, occasion, parent-brand structure, or benefit are the usual reasons.
  • Rivals = top 3 brands where focal's sharing is lowest relative to the column average. Direct substitutes — people who buy focal tend NOT to buy these brands, and vice versa.

Important: “partner” doesn't mean “the brand we share the most buyers with in absolute terms” — that's usually just the biggest brand in the category, because everyone shares with the leader. It means the brand we share with more than the rest of the field does.

Reading partitions. A market partition is a cluster of brands that share more buyers with each other than the law predicts. In the Deviations matrix, partitions show up as a rectangular block of green cells. Per Dawes (2016), the usual drivers are functional, not image-based:

  • Parent-brand partitions — sub-brands of the same parent (Coke / Diet Coke / Coke Zero).
  • Product-format partitions — caffeinated vs decaf in coffee; regular vs sensitive in toothpaste.
  • Benefit / occasion partitions — brands positioned for a specific use occasion cluster together.

The Partition candidate banner above the table flags brands with mutual positive deviations >10pp with at least two others — a heuristic for surfacing partition candidates without manual inspection. Confirm visually in the Deviations matrix and the Cluster map below.

What to look for

  • Defendable partitions — if the focal sits inside a high-deviation block with 2–3 other brands, you have a sub-segment to defend. Investigate what drives the cohabitation (format? occasion? benefit?) and protect the distinctive asset.
  • Outsider position — if the focal under-shares with the cluster the market expects, you may be priced out, distributed-out, or positioned outside the dominant occasion. Read alongside Mental Availability to diagnose whether it's a memory-structure problem or a physical-availability problem.
  • Heavy crossover with one rival — a single column with a large positive deviation tells you who the focal really competes against day-to-day. The Cluster map below visualises these partner / rival relationships geometrically.

Methodology. D is fitted by no-intercept OLS on the off-diagonal cells: D = Σ(observed × partner-penetration) ÷ Σ(partner-penetration2). Expected[x, y] = D × partner-penetration. Deviation[x, y] = observed[x, y] − expected[x, y] in percentage points. MAD = mean of |deviation| across off-diagonal cells. The Partners/Rivals card uses a separate deviation: focal's row entry in column B minus the mean of column B across the other (non-diagonal) brand rows.

References. Dawes, J. (2016). Testing the robustness of brand partitions identified from purchase duplication analysis. Journal of Business Research 69(2). Anesbury, Z., Page, B., Driesener, C., & Dawes, J. (2021). How persistent are duplication of purchase partitions?

portfolio_overview.title: How to read this page
portfolio_overview.text:
What this page shows. A one-screen snapshot of how the focal brand sits across every category in the portfolio. The headline KPI cards summarise reach (penetration), demand (volume share), and presence (mental availability). The horizontal bar chart compares the brand’s awareness to the category average in each category, and the table underneath gives the full set of brand-versus-category-average numbers.

How it is calculated. Each category’s figures come from the underlying Cat Buying and Mental Availability outputs for that category. The Overview tab does no new analysis — it stitches together the category-level numbers and ranks them so you can see at a glance where the brand is strongest and weakest.

How to use it.

  • Start here when you open the Portfolio tab. Treat the cards as a thirty-second briefing.
  • Use the bar chart to spot categories where the brand’s awareness is well above or well below the category average — those are the categories that deserve attention first.
  • Drill into Footprint, Competitive Set, Category Context, or Extension to investigate why a category looks the way it does.

portfolio_footprint.title: How to read this page
portfolio_footprint.text:
What this page shows. A grid of every brand (rows) against every category (columns). Each cell is the percentage of that category's buyers who say they are aware of the brand — i.e. how widely the brand is known inside that category. The focal brand is pinned to the top of the table.

How it is calculated. Awareness numerator comes from the upfront "which of these brands have you heard of?" question. Denominator is the set of past-12-month category buyers for each category, taken from the category screener ("which of the following food and grocery categories have you personally bought... in the last 12 months?" / "and which of these have you personally bought in the last 3 months?", depending on the portfolio timeframe). This is a purely mental-availability view — no purchase frequency, no volume share, no SCR data feeds into these cells. A dash (—) means the brand wasn't shown in that category's awareness battery, not that awareness is zero. The heatmap option shades each cell by where it sits in its column (within-category): dark = top of the category by awareness, faint = bottom. Show counts replaces the percentage with the underlying weighted respondent count.

How to use it.

  • Read across the focal-brand row to see the shape of the portfolio — which categories the brand is well-known in, which it has a foothold in, and which it is essentially absent from.
  • Sort by any column to surface the most-known brands in that category.
  • Use the brand chips to limit the table to a competitive set you care about.
  • Awareness here uses the wide category-buyer base; the same brand may show a different awareness % on its per-category deep-dive tab, where the base is the smaller focal-cat sample. Both are correct.
  • Pair a strong-in-X / weak-in-Y reading with the Competitive Set tab to see which competitor brands are crowding the mental space in each category.

portfolio_constellation.title: How to read this page
portfolio_constellation.context: Portfolio — Competitive Set sub-tab — bottom callout
portfolio_constellation.text:
What this page shows. One co-awareness network per category. Each node is a brand in that category; each line is the co-awareness overlap between two brands — how much their awareness audiences sit on top of each other. The focal brand is pinned with a halo; thicker lines = stronger overlap with the focal. Use the chips above the chart to switch categories.

How it is calculated. For every pair of brands within a category we compute the Jaccard similarity of their awareness sets — the people who said they had heard of each brand on the upfront "which of these brands have you heard of?" question: (people aware of both) ÷ (people aware of either).
