The result is a number between 0 (no shared awares) and 1 (the two brands are known by exactly the same people). The chart draws the strongest pairs as edges and uses a force-directed layout so clusters of brands that share audiences sit visually close together.

What the Jaccard score means in plain English. The number next to each rival in the side panel is a Jaccard, shown as a 0–1 decimal (e.g. 0.56). It is a symmetric overlap, not a percentage of one brand's awares. The actual interpretation: “Of all the people in this category who are aware of either brand A or brand B, what proportion are aware of both?” So 0.56 between IPK and Knorr means 56% of everyone who knows either IPK or Knorr knows both — the same number whichever brand you call the focal. 0.80 = the two brands share almost the same audience and you compete head-on for that attention; 0.20 = the two awareness sets are largely separate. A common misread is “0.56 = 56% of IPK awares know Knorr” — that conditional number lives on the Duplication of Awareness table further down the page; it is a related but different statistic.

How to use it.

  • Read down the side panel for each category — the top of the list is the focal’s mental-space competitive set in that category.
  • Compare the same focal across categories using the chips: the rivals can change quite a lot. A brand can be your #1 competitor in pasta sauces and an also-ran in pour-over sauces because awareness audiences differ.
  • Use the focal selector to flip the lens onto any competitor and ask “who shares their mental space?”
  • Trust the highlighted edges and the side-panel ranking for focal-vs-rival reads. The dot positions are useful for the wider shape of the network (clusters, isolates) but a brand can sit visually close to your focal because of its own ties to other brands, not because of its overlap with yours.

portfolio_clutter.title: How to read this page
portfolio_clutter.context: Portfolio — Category Context sub-tab — bottom callout
portfolio_clutter.text:
What this page shows. A scatter where each dot is a category, plotted on two axes that describe the state of the focal brand's mental space in that category. The X-axis is awareness set size — how many brands the average buyer in that category can recall (further right = more crowded mental space). The Y-axis is the focal brand's share of awareness — the proportion of all brand-awareness mentions in the category that go to the focal. Dot size scales with category penetration: bigger dots = more category buyers.

How it is calculated. Awareness set size = mean number of brands recalled per category buyer on the "which of these brands have you heard of?" question. Focal share of awareness = focal brand awareness ÷ sum of every brand's awareness in that category. Reference lines at the median of each axis split the chart into four quadrants.

How to read the four quadrants. The labels describe the structural state of the focal brand in each category — they deliberately don't prescribe action, because the right next step depends on the category's role in the brand's portfolio (key line vs peripheral, growth bet vs cash cow) which the chart can't see.

Top-left — Dominant
Few brands compete for awareness and the focal owns a big slice of it.
Top-right — Contested
Many brands compete and the focal still pulls a high share. Winning ground in a busy mental space.
Bottom-left — Open space
Few brands compete for awareness, but the focal doesn't yet have much of it. Could be a small or under-developed category, or one the focal simply hasn't built awareness in. Read alongside dot size (category penetration) before drawing conclusions.
Bottom-right — Crowded out
Many brands compete and the focal has a small share of awareness. The focal is in this category but not winning the awareness fight. Whether to invest harder, fix mental availability, or accept the position is a strategic call that depends on the category's importance — the chart can't make that call.

How to use it. Use the X- and Y-axis range inputs to zoom into a sub-region of the chart. Hover any dot to see the category name, set size, focal share, category penetration, and quadrant.

demographics.title: How to read this page
demographics.context: Demographics tab — bottom callout below the matrix
demographics.text:
What this page shows. A quick comparison of the focal brand's buyers against the category and the rest of the brand set on each demographic question (age, gender, LSM, household composition, etc.). One card per question; the matrix lays out the answer options as rows and the brands as columns, with the focal brand pinned in column two and the category average alongside.

Two cell-metric modes (radio at the top of the panel):

Penetration
Each cell = % of respondents in that demographic option who bought the brand in the target window. Reads as “what share of, say, 25–34 year olds bought this brand?”
Share of buyers
Each cell = % of that brand's buyers who sit in that demographic option. Each brand column sums to 100% down the options. Reads as “what does this brand's buyer base look like demographically?”

How to read it

  • Use the Focal brand dropdown to swap which brand sits in column two; switching brands re-renders every card in lock-step.
  • The chart toggle on each card flips to a horizontal-bar view. In Penetration mode the bar is the focal's % in each demographic option and the dashed marker is the focal's overall category penetration — bars longer than the marker mean focal over-performs in that demographic. In Share of buyers mode the bar is the focal's share in each option and the marker is the share of the same option in the category sample — bars longer than the marker mean focal over-represents that demographic vs the population.
  • Pin the card: a popover lets you tick Table, Chart, or both — whichever you want to send to the report deck.
  • Brand chips hide or reveal columns; Hide all / Show all flips the whole set.
  • Heatmap tints cells by their gap to the category average for the same option (blue = above, red = below). Toggle n counts to read the underlying respondent count behind each percentage.

What this page is — and is not. A headline comparison only: focal vs category vs the rest of the brand set on the demographic questions in the survey. Confidence intervals were dropped to keep the read uncluttered. For full demographic exploration — significance testing, banner cuts, weighted index calculations and so on — use the Tabs module.

Methodology. Cells are weighted when weights are configured. The Cat avg column is the share of the full screened sample (or the configured base) in the same demographic option. Bar widths in the chart are normalised to the largest value in the row so a 25% option doesn't get drowned out by a 75% one.

portfolio_extension.title: How to read this page
portfolio_extension.text:
What this page shows. A scatter of every category the focal brand is not currently in (or is small in). The X-axis measures how attractive the category is — its size, growth and ease of entry — and the Y-axis estimates how easily the focal brand’s existing meanings would translate into that category (its mental-availability fit). Bubble size scales with category penetration so you can tell at a glance which opportunities are worth real money.

How it is calculated. Category attractiveness is a composite of the category’s overall penetration, average purchase frequency, and growth signal. Brand fit is computed from the focal brand’s existing CEP and attribute network: categories where the focal’s strongest associations match the buying situations in that category score higher. Both axes are scaled 0–100 so categories from very different parts of the portfolio sit on the same chart.

How to read the four quadrants.

Top-right — Pursue
Attractive category, brand fits naturally. The obvious extensions.
Top-left — Stretch
Brand fits, category is small. Worth a small bet but not the headline play.
Bottom-right — Re-frame
Big category, fit is weak. You’d need to invest in new associations before going in. Probably more cost than it is worth.
Bottom-left — Avoid
Small category, weak fit. Skip.

How to use it. Use the axis range inputs to focus on a specific quadrant. Hover any dot for the underlying category numbers. Pair this view with Cat Buying’s Duplication of Purchase to check whether candidates already share buyers with the focal — easier extensions if they do.
